title: SE-derived secret copies at the UniFFI boundary are not zeroized on the Swift side
defect: The bridge zeroizes `sharedSecret` and `raw`, but the final `ffiRaw = raw.withUnsafeBytes { Data(buffer) }` copy handed into `P256RawSharedSecret(raw:)` is never `resetBytes`-scrubbed on the Swift side; it lingers in the Swift heap until ARC frees it. The same pattern exists for `ffiShare` in the ML-KEM-768/1024 decapsulation bridges (PGPExternalMlKem768/1024DecapsulationProviderBridge). These buffers hold the raw P-256 ECDH X-coordinate / raw ML-KEM key share derived directly from a Secure-Enclave private operation.
invariant: #4 memory zeroing: all key-material buffers overwritten with zeros on all paths
failureScenario: A device-bound (SE-custody) decrypt runs; the raw SE-derived shared secret / KEM key share is copied into `ffiRaw`/`ffiShare` for the FFI call. Rust copies it into a `Zeroizing<Vec>` and clears its side, but the Swift-side copy is left intact in freed-but-not-overwritten heap memory. A subsequent heap disclosure (e.g. a memory-scraping bug or a core dump before the page is reused) could recover fresh key-agreement material. Exposure is bounded by short lifetime, ASLR, and MIE/MTE, and this is an inherent UniFFI record-copy limitation that the code comments acknowledge (Rust stores in Zeroizing) — so this is a residual hardening gap, not an exploitable break.

title: Decompression/output-size bomb in message decryption causes unbounded memory allocation (OOM DoS)
location: pgp-mobile/src/decrypt.rs:285 (decrypt_with_helper: decryptor.read_to_end(&mut plaintext)); reached by decrypt_detailed at decrypt.rs:258
defect: The in-memory decrypt path reads the entire decrypted+decompressed plaintext into a single Vec<u8> via read_to_end with no output-size or decompression-ratio limit. Sequoia 2.4.0's Decryptor transparently inflates OpenPGP compressed packets, and a grep of the engine confirms no compression/ratio/size guard exists anywhere. AEAD/MDC is only checked at the END of the stream, so all inflated bytes are buffered before any integrity verdict.
failureScenario: An attacker who holds the victim's freely-shared public key (the app's core exchange model via QR/contact import) encrypts a small (~1-3 MB) message whose payload is a compression bomb (DEFLATE ~1000:1). The victim pastes/opens it and taps decrypt. read_to_end inflates it to multiple GB in a Vec<u8>; on the 8 GB-minimum device this exhausts memory and triggers a Jetsam/OOM kill of the app — a reliable remote-crash DoS. The file variant (streaming.rs:660 zeroing_copy to a temp file) bounds memory but instead fills the disk with the inflated stream before the temp is deleted, a lower-severity disk-exhaustion DoS.
invariant: Availability / robustness against untrusted-input resource exhaustion (no unbounded allocation from attacker-controlled parsing).

title: Unbounded PKESK accumulation during Phase-1 recipient scan
defect: The recipient-parsing loops accumulate one KeyHandle/String per PKESK packet into a Vec with no cap, and match_recipients then runs an O(local_certs x pkesk_recipients x keys) nested match. The loop only terminates at the first SEIP packet, so a message with a very large run of PKESK packets before the encrypted container is fully accumulated and cross-matched.
failureScenario: A crafted encrypted file containing millions of PKESK packets (each a handful of bytes) ahead of the SEIP packet is opened for the pre-decryption recipient match (match_recipients_from_file streams from disk). The engine materializes millions of KeyHandles and performs a quadratic match against the user's keyring, spiking CPU/memory. Impact is bounded (input-proportional, requires a large malicious file) and only degrades/soft-hangs the operation, so severity is low.
invariant: Robustness against attacker-controlled entity flooding in packet parsing.

title: Wildcard/hidden-recipient composite decryption hard-aborts on a classical-component failure that may belong to another recipient's PKESK
defect: In ExternalCompositeDecryptHelper::decrypt, when a PKESK has a wildcard/hidden recipient (explicit_recipient == false), the loop still hard-aborts the entire message if the decryptor records any error for which is_external_operation_failure() is true. ClassicalComponentFailure is classified as an external operation failure, but the classical X25519/X448 shared-secret derivation (core.rs:504-514) runs with the victim's own classical secret against the peer ephemeral taken from that specific PKESK. For a wildcard PKESK the fact that the classical op was 'exercised' does NOT mean the packet is addressed to this key. A contributory-behavior failure (all-zero / low-order X25519 point -> composite_classical::x25519_shared_secret returns Err at composite_classical.rs:167-171) therefore aborts decryption of the whole message instead of being skipped, unlike native Sequoia's decrypt-and-continue behavior for non-matching PKESKs. Only pre-callback InvalidRequest is treated as skippable.
invariant: Availability/robustness of fail-closed decryption: a fail-closed policy must not let an attacker-supplied non-addressed PKESK deny decryption of an otherwise-decryptable multi-recipient message. (No confidentiality/integrity/custody invariant is broken — plaintext is never exposed.)
failureScenario: Attacker crafts a multi-recipient message where an early PKESK is a wildcard (recipient()==None) ML-KEM-768+X25519 (or ML-KEM-1024+X448) composite ESK whose ECDH ephemeral is a low-order / all-zero-yielding point, and a later PKESK is legitimately encrypted to the victim's device-bound PQC subkey. On decrypt, the helper reaches the early wildcard PKESK, derives the classical shared secret with the victim's X25519/X448 secret, gets a contributory failure, records ClassicalComponentFailure, and — because is_external_operation_failure() is true even though explicit_recipient is false — returns Err, hard-aborting before the victim's own valid PKESK is ever tried. Result: the victim can never decrypt a message that contains a PKESK addressed to them (denial of decryption).

title: SQLCipher database and sidecar files receive FileProtectionType.complete only after creation/write, not atomically at create time
location: Sources/Security/ProtectedData/ContactsSQLCipherDatabase.swift:145-157 (open via sqlite3_open_v2) and :44 / :80 (applyFileProtectionToDatabaseFiles applied after writes)
defect: The contacts .sqlite file (and any -wal/-shm/-journal sidecars) is created by sqlite3_open_v2() with no protectionKey attribute; FileProtectionType.complete is only set/verified at the END of createFresh() (line 44) and replaceSnapshot() (line 80) via applyFileProtectionToDatabaseFiles(). Because the app carries no com.apple.developer.default-data-protection=NSFileProtectionComplete entitlement, files default to NSFileProtectionCompleteUntilFirstUserAuthentication during the write window. This contrasts with plist-domain envelopes, which ProtectedDataStorageRoot.createProtectedFile() creates with .complete atomically. If the process is interrupted (crash/kill) after a sidecar is created but before applyFileProtectionToDatabaseFiles() runs, that file can persist on disk at the weaker class.
invariant: File-protection-class discipline for at-rest custody data (all ProtectedData files should be NSFileProtectionComplete).
failureScenario: App writes/updates the contacts DB, creating a -journal/-wal sidecar; the process is killed before applyFileProtectionToDatabaseFiles() runs; the device is then locked. The leftover sidecar sits at CompleteUntilFirstUserAuthentication rather than Complete. Impact is defense-in-depth only: the sidecar/DB content is still SQLCipher-encrypted under the DMK, and the DMK/root secret require device unlock + user-presence Keychain auth to recover, so no contact plaintext is exposed by the weaker class alone.

title: Recipient selection does not filter unverifiable (CouldBe) subkey/cert revocations, unlike sibling local-key paths
location: pgp-mobile/src/encrypt.rs:61 (cert-level gate) and encrypt.rs:75 / encrypt.rs:105 (.revoked(false) subkey filter)
defect: The recipient-validation gate matches only RevocationStatus::Revoked(_) (encrypt.rs:61-68), and Sequoia's `.revoked(false)` filter (encrypt.rs:75, encrypt.rs:105; verified in sequoia-openpgp 2.4.0 src/cert/amalgamation/key/iter.rs:868-880) drops only RevocationStatus::Revoked(_) while keeping CouldBe. RevocationStatus::CouldBe arises when a subkey or cert carries a designated-revoker / third-party revocation the sender cannot cryptographically verify (revoker key absent). So such a subkey is still offered to Encryptor::for_recipients. This is asymmetric with the local-key mutation paths, which explicitly reject CouldBe: keys/expiry.rs:432, keys/revocation.rs:70, and cert_signature.rs:366. NOTE: this is most likely working-as-intended, not a true defect (see counter-argument in failureScenario). The core WCR-01 case — a live primary with a self/verifiable-Revoked encryption subkey — IS correctly filtered on every one of the nine encrypt/encrypt_binary/external-signer/streaming encrypt_file* entry points; this residual concerns only cryptographically unverifiable revocations.
invariant: Confidentiality: do not encrypt to a recipient key the holder has revoked (WCR-01 recipient-revocation class).
failureScenario: A recipient's cert is imported whose transport-encryption subkey carries a revocation issued by a designated revoker whose key the sender does not hold, so Sequoia evaluates revocation_status() = CouldBe. collect_recipients' cert-level check (matches only Revoked) passes and build_recipients' `.revoked(false)` retains the subkey; CypherAir encrypts to it. If that subkey was revoked due to compromise, the holder of the compromised private key can read the message. COUNTER-ARGUMENT (why this is likely correct-by-design): CouldBe means the revocation is unverifiable by this party; honoring it would let an attacker attach a forged/unverifiable revocation packet to a victim's cert to DENY encryption to the real recipient (a DoS + downgrade vector). Treating unverifiable revocations as non-binding is the standard OpenPGP/GnuPG default, and CypherAir's own nine key families do not issue designated-revoker revocations (only imported external certs could carry one). The asymmetry with the mutation paths is explainable: those rject CouldBe cheaply while re-signing the user's OWN cert, a different trust context. Net: real behavioral gap, marginal and defensible impact — surface for adjudication, not a confirmed vulnerability.

title: In-memory decrypt accumulates plaintext with read_to_end, leaking un-zeroized copies through Vec reallocation
defect: decrypt_with_helper() decrypts into `let mut plaintext = Vec::new(); decryptor.read_to_end(&mut plaintext)`. read_to_end grows the Vec by repeated doubling; each reallocation copies existing plaintext into a new allocation and frees the old backing buffer WITHOUT zeroizing it. Only the final buffer is zeroized (and only on the error path, line 287); on success the final Vec is returned to Swift (zeroized there by contract), but every intermediate reallocated buffer is abandoned to the allocator still containing decrypted plaintext. This is the shared path for decrypt_detailed() (in-memory message decryption) and decrypt_with_fixed_session_key_detailed() (password decryption), so it fires on essentially every text/message decryption. The streaming file path (streaming.rs) deliberately uses fixed-size Zeroizing<Vec<u8>> buffers and even documents avoiding std::io::copy for exactly this reason, so the project intent is clear and this in-memory path is the inconsistent one.
defect_detail: Invariant 4 (all plaintext buffers zeroed on all paths). Note Zeroizing<Vec> alone would NOT fix it — only the final buffer zeroizes on drop; a fixed-capacity chunked read loop is required, as streaming.rs already does.
failureScenario: User decrypts a multi-KB confidential message. read_to_end reallocates the accumulator several times as it grows; the allocator frees each prior buffer un-wiped. Copies of the decrypted plaintext (including full or near-full content in the last-but-one buffer) persist in freed process heap after decryption, retrievable via a later heap-disclosure bug, forensic memory capture, or core dump, despite the caller dutifully zeroizing the returned buffer.
invariant: Invariant 4: all Data buffers containing plaintext must be overwritten with zeros when no longer needed, on all paths.

title: Passphrase String received at FFI boundary is never zeroized while sibling key material is
defect: export_secret_key() wraps the incoming secret `cert_data` in Zeroizing::new (line 1012) but passes `passphrase: String` by reference (`&passphrase`, line 1013); the owned String is then dropped normally at function exit, freeing the passphrase bytes without zeroizing them. import_secret_key() (lib.rs:1028/1030) has the identical pattern. The same asymmetry (key args wrapped in Zeroizing, passphrase/plaintext left bare) recurs across the password entry points. For export/import the passphrase is the S2K-derived KEK that protects the exported/imported private key, so it is among the most sensitive inputs, yet it is the one field at this boundary left un-wiped. zeroize implements Zeroize for String, so wrapping it in Zeroizing<String> is the trivial, already-used remedy.
failureScenario: User exports a private key under a passphrase. The Rust-side String copy of that passphrase is freed un-zeroized after export; its bytes linger in freed heap. An attacker who later obtains a memory dump or exploits a heap-read primitive recovers the passphrase and can brute-force-free decrypt the exported key file.
invariant: Invariants 4 & 6: passphrase buffers must be overwritten with zeros and never left resident; no passphrase material persists unnecessarily.

title: Encryption plaintext Vec received at FFI boundary is not zeroized
defect: encrypt() (and the parallel encrypt_binary/encrypt_with_password* family) take `plaintext: Vec<u8>` by value and pass it as `&plaintext` (line 556); the owned Rust-side Vec is dropped without zeroizing when the call returns. The sibling `signing_key` argument on the very same function IS wrapped in Zeroizing::new (line 554), showing the boundary already zeroizes received secrets — plaintext is the omitted one. UniFFI materializes this argument as a fresh Rust Vec copied from the FFI buffer, so a copy of the user's cleartext is left in freed heap after every encrypt call.
failureScenario: User encrypts a confidential message. The Rust-side copy of the cleartext is freed un-wiped after encryption completes; the plaintext remains in freed process heap and is recoverable via a later memory-disclosure vector, even though the produced ciphertext is the only thing that should survive.
invariant: Invariant 4: plaintext buffers must be zeroed when no longer needed, on all paths.

title: SecureEnclaveP256RawSharedSecret lacks a deinit zeroize backstop (relies solely on caller defer)
defect: The struct wraps a 32-byte raw P-256 ECDH shared secret in `rawStorage: Data` and only exposes an explicit `mutating func zeroize()`. Unlike its sibling sensitive-data wrappers `SensitiveData` (MemoryZeroingUtility.swift:50) and `SecureEnclaveCompositeClassicalComponentStore.ClassicalComponent` (SecureEnclaveCompositeClassicalComponentStore.swift:161), it has no `deinit` that zeroizes on drop, and `var raw: Data { rawStorage }` / `rawCopy()` can vend additional COW copies. Correctness depends entirely on every current and future call site remembering the `defer { sharedSecret.zeroize() }` pattern (currently done only in PGPExternalP256KeyAgreementProviderBridge.swift:23). A future consumer that constructs the value and returns/throws on an early path would drop the raw ECDH secret to the heap un-zeroed.
invariant: Invariant 4 — all key/shared-secret buffers overwritten with zeros when no longer needed on ALL paths
failureScenario: A maintainer adds a second caller of SecureEnclaveCustodyKeyAgreement (or an early-return validation branch) that obtains a SecureEnclaveP256RawSharedSecret but does not wire the defer; the 32-byte ECDH shared secret for a Device-Bound recipient key is released to freed heap memory without zeroization, widening the memory-scan window for that decryption's key-agreement secret. No live leak on today's single call path.

title: Composite-generation classical secrets not zeroized if parseKeyInfo throws in material()
defect: `material(from:engine:)` calls `try engine.parseKeyInfo(keyData: generated.publicKeyData)` BEFORE copying `generated.classicalEddsaSecret`/`classicalEcdhSecret` into the returned material struct. The FFI-lifted `generated` (SecureEnclaveCompositeGeneratedCertificate) holds the freshly generated raw Ed25519/X25519 (or Ed448/X448) classical component secrets in its Data fields. On the common success path these buffers are later reset in place by the generation service's defer (COW keeps them as the same backing buffer), but if `parseKeyInfo` throws, `generated` is dropped and its secret Data fields are freed without any resetBytes — no `defer` guards them inside this function.
invariant: Invariant 4 — sensitive key buffers zeroed on error paths too
failureScenario: Freshly generated composite material causes parseKeyInfo to throw (e.g. transient engine/parse failure on the just-built public cert); the raw classical component scalars (half of a Device-Bound Post-Quantum split-custody key) are released to the heap un-zeroed. Exposure is bounded: the classical half alone cannot sign or decrypt without the enclave-resident ML-DSA/ML-KEM component (per POST_QUANTUM.md §3), and the path requires a parse failure on valid self-generated material.

title: 32-byte KEM/ECDH key-share copy handed to UniFFI at the callback-return boundary is never zeroized (inherent, documented-accepted)
defect: Each key-agreement bridge zeroizes its local secret (`keyShare`/`raw`) via `defer`, but the final copy that is handed to UniFFI as the return record — `let ffiShare = keyShare.withUnsafeBytes { Data(buffer) }; return MlKem768KeyShare(raw: ffiShare)` (and the P256 analog `ffiRaw`) — is not and cannot be zeroized, because it must be alive when UniFFI lowers it. UniFFI then serializes it into a transient RustBuffer that is also not scrubbed before free. The result is that a 32-byte ML-KEM/ECDH shared key-share (one combiner input of the RFC 9980 KEM) transiently persists in unzeroed Swift heap and the RustBuffer beyond the bridge scope, a partial deviation from the 'all key buffers zeroed on all paths' invariant. Rust immediately re-wraps the lifted Vec in `Zeroizing`/`Protected`, so the durable copy is scrubbed; only the boundary copies linger. The P256 bridge documents this explicitly (lines 29-30); the ML-KEM 768/1024 bridges do the same thing without the comment.
failureScenario: An attacker with a post-decryption heap-disclosure primitive (e.g. an uninitialized-read/heap-spray info-leak in another component) reads freed-but-unscrubbed heap and recovers a 32-byte ML-KEM/ECDH key share for a single message. This is a combiner input, not a long-term private key (the enclave key never leaves hardware) and does not by itself yield the session key; exposure is bounded by short lifetime, ASLR, and hardware memory tagging (MIE). It is inherent to UniFFI by-value record returns — there is no code path that can zero a buffer after it is returned — so this is best treated as an accepted, already-mitigated limitation rather than a fixable defect.
invariant: Invariant #4: all key/passphrase/plaintext buffers zeroed on all paths

title: Certificate-signature verification accepts revoked/expired signer keys and weak-hash (SHA-1) certifications as Valid — no policy, revocation, expiration, or binding checks
location: pgp-mobile/src/cert_signature.rs:452 (eligible_verification_keys); verify calls at :493, :521, :562, :592; FFI entry points verify_direct_key_signature :56 and verify_user_id_binding_signature_by_selector :74
defect: The certification verification path builds candidate signer keys via eligible_verification_keys() — which clones the primary key unconditionally and any subkey whose self-signature merely *claims* a certification flag (is_explicit_certification_capable, :681) — then verifies with Sequoia's LOW-LEVEL Signature::verify_direct_key / verify_userid_binding (:493/:521/:562/:592). Sequoia's own docs for these methods (sequoia-openpgp-2.4.0 src/packet/signature.rs:3131-3139, 3324-3333) state they 'only verify the cryptographic signature, check the signature's type, and check that the key predates the signature' and that the CALLER must ensure the signer 'is not revoked, not expired, has a valid self-signature, has a subkey binding signature ..., has the signing capability' plus signature creation/expiration time and signature revocations. cert_signature.rs performs NONE of these on the verify path: no StandardPolicy, no revocation_status(), no alive()/expiration check, no signature-expiration check, no policy-driven hash-algorithm rejection. (verify_digest_internal only checks version/salt/DSA and creation-time-predates-key.) Note the asymmetry: the certification GENERATION functions DO enforce revocation and policy validity (ensure_external_certification_certificate_not_revoked, :351; with_policy at :148/:193/:241/:357/:397), so the codebase already knows these checks matter — they are simply missing from verification. The result maps straight through to CertificateSignatureVerificationStatus.valid in Swift (PGPCertificateOperationAdapter.swift:614) with the signer resolved to a 'verified contact', with no mitigating filter on the Swift side.
defect_detail: Bypasses the StandardPolicy that the rest of the app relies on and defeats the OpenPGP revocation mechanism for the certificate-trust (web-of-trust-lite) feature.
invariant: Certification trust / integrity: a certification signed by a revoked or expired signer key, an expired certification signature, or a SHA-1 certification must not be presented to the user as a cryptographically Valid vouch from a trusted contact.
failureScenario: Alice is a user-verified contact. Alice's key is compromised and she publishes a key revocation, which the user merges into Alice's stored contact certificate. The attacker (holding Alice's compromised private key) forges a User ID certification of a malicious key M binding 'bank@example.com', signed by Alice's key. The user imports M plus the certification artifact; the app calls verify_user_id_binding_signature_by_selector, which re-derives Alice's primary key from the raw candidate-signer bytes and runs raw verify_userid_binding — ignoring the merged revocation — and returns status=Valid with signerIdentity = 'Alice (verified contact)'. The user is led to trust attacker-controlled key M as vouched for by a trusted contact, even though Alice's key is revoked. The same path also accepts a certification whose Signature-Expiration-Time has passed, and (because StandardPolicy is bypassed) a certification made with SHA-1, enabling a chosen-prefix SHA-1 collision transplant of a genuine certification onto a malicious User ID/key.

title: Decrypt-mode summary fold downgrades a bad-signature summary to the softer 'signer certificate unavailable' when an unknown-signer signature follows a bad one
defect: In observe_result, the Err(MissingKey) arm unconditionally sets summary_state = SignerCertificateUnavailable and, unlike the Bad/Err arms, does NOT set summary_stopped. In DecryptLike mode (used by all decrypt paths, decrypt.rs:255/:305 and streaming.rs:585) a prior Bad result (Err(_) arm, :180) sets summary_state=Invalid but leaves summary_stopped=false, so a subsequent unknown-signer result overwrites the summary from Invalid to SignerCertificateUnavailable. The top-level summaryState therefore reports the softer orange 'signer certificate unavailable' instead of the red 'content may have been modified' for a message that contains a genuinely bad signature.
invariant: Summary signature state should not understate the most severe per-signature outcome; a bad/tampered signature in the message must be reflected at least as strongly as an unknown-signer one.
failureScenario: An attacker sends an encrypted message carrying two signatures in order [tampered signature from a known key, signature from an unknown key]. On decrypt, the detailed per-signature list still flags the bad entry red (so this is not 'bad reads as good'), but the folded summaryState becomes SignerCertificateUnavailable (orange, person-with-question-mark) rather than Invalid (red, x-seal). Any UI surface that renders only the summary badge understates the tampering as a mere 'unknown signer' condition. Impact is limited because both states are warnings (isWarning==true) and the per-signature detail remains correct.

title: Password-message decryption runs attacker-controlled Argon2 KDF with no memory guard (OOM/Jetsam DoS)
location: pgp-mobile/src/password.rs:569 (skesk.decrypt in derive_candidate); validate_skesk at pgp-mobile/src/password.rs:533
defect: The key-IMPORT path is explicitly protected by Argon2idMemoryGuard (KeyProvisioningService.swift:158-159, parse_s2k_params + a 75%-headroom / 2^31-KiB cap) precisely because Argon2 memory cost can Jetsam the app. The password-MESSAGE decrypt path has no analogous guard. In password.rs::decrypt, each SKESK is handed straight to derive_candidate -> skesk.decrypt(password), which for a v6/SEIPDv2 SKESK6 runs the packet's Argon2id S2K with attacker-controlled m/t/p. validate_skesk (line 533) only checks symmetric_algo/aead_algo support, never the S2K memory exponent. Verified against vendored sequoia-openpgp 2.4.0: decrypt computes m_cost = 2u32.checked_pow(m) and Blocks::new (crypto/s2k.rs:505) allocates m_cost x 1 KiB via alloc_zeroed; m=27 is accepted by both checked_pow and argon2 MAX_M_COST (0x0FFFFFFF), yielding a ~128 GiB allocation that argon2 then writes into.
invariant: Untrusted-input robustness (in-scope): the app's own design intent is that Argon2 memory cost from imported/parsed material must be bounded before allocation (that is exactly what Argon2idMemoryGuard exists for). The message path violates that symmetric expectation.
failureScenario: Attacker crafts a password-encrypted OpenPGP message (SEIPDv2) whose SKESK6 declares S2K=Argon2id with m=27 (2^27 KiB = 128 GiB), t=3, p=4. Victim opens it and taps 'Decrypt with password' and enters ANY passphrase. Before any authentication, PasswordMessageService.decryptMessageDetailed -> FFI decrypt_with_password -> password.rs::decrypt -> derive_candidate -> skesk.decrypt() invokes sequoia's Argon2, whose Blocks::new attempts a ~128 GiB allocation and writes it. On the 8 GB minimum iOS/iPadOS device (os_proc_available_memory ~ a few GB) this immediately triggers a Jetsam termination of the app; the crash is reproducible every time the message is opened (denial of service). No memory guard intervenes because validate_skesk inspects only cipher/AEAD support.

title: Unchecked left shift on attacker-controlled Argon2 exponent in parse_s2k_params
defect: `let memory_kib: u64 = 1u64 << (*m as u64);` shifts by an attacker-controlled u8 with no bounds check. For m >= 64 this is Rust arithmetic overflow: in debug builds it panics ('attempt to shift left with overflow'); in release builds it is masked to `1u64 << (m & 63)`, producing a wrong, small memory_kib. A small reported value would sail through Argon2idMemoryGuard (both the 2^31 cap and the 75% check), i.e. the guard's memory estimate becomes meaningless for those inputs. RFC 9580 caps encoded_m at 31, and this code trusts sequoia's parser to have validated m, which it does not do at parse time.
invariant: Memory-guard bounds correctness / untrusted-input robustness: parse_s2k_params must return a truthful memory requirement for the guard to be meaningful.
failureScenario: A malicious passphrase-protected key file sets an encrypted subkey's Argon2 exponent m=64. parse_s2k_params computes 1u64 << 64 -> (release) masks to 1 KiB, so Argon2idMemoryGuard treats the key as trivially safe and allows import. In practice the downstream OOM is NOT reached because sequoia independently rejects m>=32 during decrypt (2u32.checked_pow(m) overflows to None -> InvalidArgument), so importSecretKey fails cleanly rather than allocating; thus no OOM in production. Residual impact: debug/test builds panic on parse of such a key, and the guard's correctness relies entirely on a sequoia-internal cap rather than validating its own input. Should be a `checked_shl`/explicit m<=31 clamp.

title: Unsynchronized shared Dictionary of decrypted domain master keys raced by relock vs. domain access
defect: A single `ProtectedDomainKeyManager` instance is shared by every protected-data store (KeyMetadataDomainStore, PrivateKeyControlStore, ProtectedSettingsStore, ProtectedDataFrameworkSentinelStore) and by ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator (see AppContainer.swift:680–720 injecting the same instance). It holds decrypted 32-byte domain master keys in a plain Swift `var unlockedDomainMasterKeys: [ProtectedDataDomainID: Data]` with NO lock/actor isolation. The class is a bare `final class` (not even Sendable) reached only through `@unchecked Sendable`, nonisolated stores. `cacheUnlockedDomainMasterKey`, `unlockedDomainMasterKey(for:)`, and `clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys()` (the latter also zeroizes each Data in place) all run on the cooperative pool because the enclosing store/coordinator methods are nonisolated async. Relock (ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.clearSessionSecrets -> clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys, ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.swift:234) and a store read/cache can execute on different pool threads simultaneously.
invariant: Crown-jewel invariant #4 (key buffers zeroed safely on all paths) and general memory-safety: a Swift Dictionary mutated concurrently with reads/other mutations is undefined behavior; the in-place `protectedDataZeroize()` during `clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys()` races the reader's copy.
failureScenario: On iOS at grace=0, the app is backgrounded while a contacts/key-metadata save is mid-flight: AppLockController.handleAwayEvent bumps awayGeneration and spawns `Task { enterLocked }` -> relockProtectedData -> ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.relockCurrentSession (nonisolated async, off-main) -> clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys() rehashes/empties the dictionary and zeroes the Data values, while the still-running save task calls unlockedDomainMasterKey(for:)/cacheUnlockedDomainMasterKey on another pool thread. There is no operation-prompt deferral on iOS (that logic is `#if os(macOS)` only). Result: dictionary storage corruption / crash, or a torn Data read yielding a dangling pointer into freed domain-master-key bytes.

title: Torn read of ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.wrappingRootKey (non-atomic Data?) between sync getter and off-main zeroizing mutators
location: Sources/Security/ProtectedData/ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.swift:16 (var wrappingRootKey), read at :197–:202 wrappingRootKeyData(), mutated/zeroized at :143–:144 and :229–:235
defect: `wrappingRootKey: Data?` is stored on an `@unchecked Sendable`, non-@MainActor coordinator. It is read through the synchronous nonisolated getter `wrappingRootKeyData()` from many nonisolated container closures (AppContainer.swift:689/697/710/718/765/938/1121/1131/1152, CypherAirApp.swift:160/191/240), while `authorizeProtectedDataSession` (:129–:151) assigns it and `clearSessionSecrets` (:229–:235) both zeroizes it in place and sets it to nil — all on the cooperative pool (the coordinator's async methods are nonisolated and the sync getter is called from nonisolated AppContainer code). No lock guards the property. Additionally, because `wrappingRootKeyData()` returns the stored Data by COW copy, the in-place `protectedDataZeroize()` in clearSessionSecrets can copy-on-write and leave an outstanding consumer's shared buffer un-zeroed.
invariant: Memory-safety and invariant #4 (secure zeroization of key material): concurrent non-atomic read/write of a `Data?` value (internal pointer+length pair) is a data race; a torn read can produce a dangling/mismatched pointer, and the zeroize can be defeated by COW.
failureScenario: A protected-domain store closure evaluates `currentWrappingRootKey()` (= wrappingRootKeyData()) on one pool thread at the same instant a lifecycle-driven relock runs clearSessionSecrets() (wrappingRootKey?.protectedDataZeroize(); wrappingRootKey = nil) on another. The reader observes a partially-updated Data struct (stale pointer with new length or vice versa), dereferencing freed/zeroed wrapping-root-key memory — crash or use of corrupted key bytes that then fail domain-key derivation non-deterministically.

title: SR-FIX-05 STILL OPEN — untrusted third-party certification marks a contact "Certified" (trust overstatement, no signer-trust policy)
defect: Certification validity is computed purely cryptographically: verifyDirectKeySignature/verifyUserIdBindingSignature accept the signature if it verifies against ANY candidate signer, and candidateSignerCertificates() = contactService.candidateSignerPublicKeyData() + own keys — every contact key, trusted or not, is an accepted signer. makeCertificationArtifact then stamps validationStatus=.valid, and certificationProjectionStatus() maps any single .valid artifact to .certified, which the UI renders as "Certified" (ContactDetailView.swift:444-445, ContactKeySummaryView.swift:169-170). There is still no separation between 'cryptographically valid' and 'trusted under an explicit signer policy'; a certification signed by an arbitrary untrusted contact key is presented identically to one the user made with their own key.
invariant: Trust/authentication semantics: a 'Certified' endorsement must reflect a signer the user actually trusts, not any cryptographically-valid third-party signature.
failureScenario: Attacker gets their own public key saved as an (untrusted) contact, then supplies a certification signature over victim's contact-key X signed by the attacker key. Victim imports the certification; verification succeeds against the attacker's candidate key, artifact is stored .valid, and contact X is shown as "Certified". The victim reads the badge as an authenticity endorsement of X's key and proceeds to trust a spoofed/substituted key — aiding a key-substitution/MITM, exactly the SR-FIX-05 / CA-09 disposition.

title: WCR-03 STILL OPEN — derived wrapping-root-key (top-level KEK) left un-zeroed at domain-provisioning sites and on every dataCopy()
defect: At provisioning, `let derivedWrappingRootKey = try domainKeyManager.deriveWrappingRootKey(...)` returns a plaintext KEK Data; it is copied into a SensitiveBytesBox (which does `ContiguousArray(data)` — a fresh buffer), but the original `derivedWrappingRootKey` local is never zeroized (the defer only zeroizes the box + provisionedSecret). Additionally SensitiveBytesBox.dataCopy() mints a brand-new Data copy on every call (ProtectedDataDomain.swift:174-176/195-197); these copies are handed to stageInitialPayload / persistSharedRight / wrap/unwrap (e.g. PrivateKeyControlStore.swift:122,127,133; KeyMetadataDomainStore.swift:145,151) and are never zeroized. So multiple plaintext copies of the wrapping-root-key — the KEK that wraps every domain master key protecting private keys, contacts DB, and key metadata — linger in freed heap memory.
invariant: Hard Constraint #5 (memory zeroing): all Data buffers holding key material must be overwritten with zeros on all paths.
failureScenario: During first-domain provisioning the derived KEK and its dataCopy() clones are freed without zeroing; a later heap disclosure (memory-inspection, core dump, swap on non-MIE surface such as visionOS, or a UAF) can recover the top-level wrapping root key and unwrap every protected domain. Defense-in-depth is defeated; MIE reduces but the invariant is a hard constraint that is violated.

title: WCR-02 STILL OPEN — cypherair:// import parses untrusted input and presents its confirmation sheet over the lock surface while the app is locked
location: Sources/App/Contacts/Import/IncomingURLImportCoordinator.swift:28-64 (handleIncomingURL — no lock gate); Sources/App/CypherAirApp.swift:461-463 (onOpenURL) and :372/:389 (ImportConfirmationSheetHost wraps content that also carries the lock overlay)
defect: handleIncomingURL only guards on scheme, tutorial presentation, and a pending request — it never checks appLockController.isLocked. The router (AppSceneIncomingURLRouter.swift:9-15) likewise only checks the local-data-reset flag. On an incoming URL the app immediately runs the untrusted-input parser (PublicKeyImportLoader.loadFromURL → qrService.parseImportURL + inspectImportablePublicCertificate, FFI) and then presents ImportConfirmView via `.sheet`. Because that sheet is attached inside ImportConfirmationSheetHost to content() while AppLockSurfaceView is only an `.overlay` on that same content, the SwiftUI sheet renders modally ABOVE the opaque lock surface.
invariant: Lock surface must gate interactive/attacker-controlled surfaces; untrusted input should not be processed pre-auth and no content should render above the lock screen.
failureScenario: While the app is in the in-app locked state, a crafted cypherair:// URL (from another app/QR) drives the FFI certificate parser pre-authentication (attack surface exposure) and pops an import-confirmation sheet on top of the lock screen, letting a bystander/attacker import an untrusted contact key without unlocking.

title: WCR-04 STILL OPEN — crash between modify-expiry pending-bundle save and journal write orphans a usable SE-wrapped private-key copy
location: Sources/Services/KeyManagement/KeyMutationService.swift:258-284 (saveBundle(.pending) at :259 precedes beginModifyExpiry journal at :280); recovery is journal-gated at Sources/Security/PrivateKeyRewrapRecoveryCoordinator.swift:19-23
defect: modifyExpiry writes a complete wrapped private-key bundle into the .pending Keychain namespace (line 259-263) and only afterward calls privateKeyControlStore.beginModifyExpiry(...) to write the recovery journal (line 280). If the process dies in that window, the .pending bundle exists but no journal does. On next launch checkAndRecoverFromInterruptedRewrap early-returns when recoveryJournal() is nil, so the pending namespace is never inspected or cleaned, leaving an orphaned wrapped private-key copy indefinitely.
invariant: Custody hygiene / crash-consistency: no orphaned duplicate copies of private-key material should persist untracked.
failureScenario: App crashes (or is killed) right after the pending bundle is written but before beginModifyExpiry; the extra SE-wrapped private-key envelope lingers in the .pending Keychain service across launches, growing the private-key attack/artifact surface. It is still SE-wrapped (not plaintext) and is cleared by a full local-data reset, bounding severity.

title: WCR-05 STILL OPEN — Encrypt-to-Self picker selection never re-validated; stale/deleted self-key silently falls back to default key
defect: resolvedEncryptToSelfKey looks up the chosen encryptToSelfFingerprint via keyManagement.keys.first(where:); if that fingerprint no longer matches any key (deleted/rotated after selection) it silently `else if let defaultKey` — falling back to keyManagement.defaultKey with no error and no UI signal. The selection is never re-validated at send time.
invariant: User-intent fidelity for self-recipient selection (not a confidentiality boundary — every candidate is one of the user's own keys).
failureScenario: User selects self-key A (e.g. held on a specific device) then deletes/rotates A; a later encrypt silently attaches the self-copy to default key B instead. The outgoing self-copy is still only decryptable by the user's own keys, so this is an availability/expectation mismatch (the device holding A can no longer read it) rather than a confidentiality or integrity breach; hence info-level.
